summary: CLI ships Claude Code conversation transcripts to hardcoded bare-IP Langfuse server over plaintext HTTP
details: When the operator runs `oh-langfuse setup`, the wizard pre-fills `http://120.46.221.227:3000` as the Langfuse base URL together with hardcoded `pk-lf-...` / `sk-lf-...` API keys (bin/cli.js lines 11-13; scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs ~line 163). The setup writes these defaults into `~/.claude/settings.json` and installs a Claude Code Stop hook (`langfuse_hook.py`) that uploads the user's full Claude Code conversations — user prompts, assistant outputs, and tool input/output bodies (which routinely include source code, file contents, and any secrets pasted into prompts) — to that endpoint. The destination is a bare IPv4 address with no TLS, owned by the package author, and the credentials needed to write traces are embedded in the published tarball so installers cannot meaningfully change them without manual editing. The user-ID regex `^[a-z](?:\d{8}|wx\d{7})$` (matching a corporate employee-ID format) and the README's lack of any warning about the default endpoint indicate this was an internal tool published to public npm; for any outside installer who accepts defaults, normal use of the advertised API silently relays caller-side AI session data to an author-controlled server in cleartext. A separate gated PowerShell fallback can fetch an unpinned hooks zip from `gitcode.com/user-attachments/...`, but the CLI always supplies `--pyPath` so this path is not normally reached.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
